variables.content: For paramagnetic 3D-HEG calculations performed in a finite periodic simulation cell containing N = 178 electrons, the Slater-Jastrow (SJ) trial wave function—defined as a single Slater determinant of plane-wave orbitals multiplied by a Jastrow correlation factor—and the Slater-Jastrow-backflow (SJB) trial wave function—the SJ form with additional backflow-transformed quasiparticle coordinates—yield very similar diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) quasiparticle effective masses m*. Specifically, at density parameter r_s = 1 (r_s = ((3/(4π n))^{1/3} a_B^{-1})), the DMC values are m* = 0.915(1) with the SJ trial WF and m* = 0.921(1) with the SJB trial WF, and at r_s = 10 the DMC values are m* = 0.75(1) (SJ) and m* = 0.78(1) (SJB); these numerical results indicate that, for the paramagnetic 3D-HEG at N = 178 and the densities studied, inclusion of backflow in the trial WF produces only small quantitative changes in the DMC effective mass.

For a three-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (3D-HEG) simulation cell containing N = 178 electrons under periodic boundary conditions, define the Slater-Jastrow (SJ) trial wave function as a single Slater determinant of plane-wave orbitals multiplied by a Jastrow correlation factor, and define the Slater-Jastrow-backflow (SJB) trial wave function as the SJ form evaluated at backflow-transformed quasiparticle coordinates that are smooth functions of all electron positions. When the Jastrow and backflow parameters are optimized first by stochastic variance minimization and then by stochastic energy minimization, the resulting trial wave functions have nodal surfaces and variational energies that are sufficiently converged that the residual fixed-node bias in diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) total-energy differences—used to compute the interacting single-particle energy band E(k) and hence the finite-N effective mass m*(N=178) = k_F / (dE/dk)_{k_F}—is negligibly small compared with the observed numerical differences between the SJ and SJB m* values at the same system size N = 178 and the stated densities.

In the Slater–Jastrow–backflow (SJB) trial wave function for an N-electron two-dimensional uniform electron liquid (2D-UEL), the particle coordinates r_i in the Slater determinant are replaced by backflow-shifted coordinates X_i = r_i + ξ_i({r_j}), where each backflow displacement ξ_i({r_j}) is represented by a parametrized function (for example a polynomial) of interelectronic separations and the full trial function is Ψ_SJB({r_i}) = exp[J({r_i})] · det[φ_t(X_j)] with J the Jastrow factor; including this inhomogeneous backflow transformation materially modifies the nodal surface used in fixed-node DMC relative to the bare Slater–Jastrow (SJ) determinant and therefore substantially alters the fixed-node bias in quasiparticle energies and their derivatives. Concretely, for paramagnetic 2D-UEL at low density r_s = 5 the authors' comparisons show that SJ-based VMC and DMC yield m* < 1 whereas SJB-based VMC and DMC yield m* > 1, demonstrating that static correlation incorporated via backflow is crucial to obtain the correct sign and magnitude of mass renormalization in these calculations.

Quantum Monte Carlo calculations for the homogeneous electron gas that employ a backflow (BF) correlated trial wave function, which introduces explicit momentum-dependent correlations beyond the Slater–Jastrow form, yield a quasiparticle renormalization factor Z_{k_F}^{HEG} = 0.66(2) at density parameter r_s ≈ 4, representing a modest reduction relative to the QMC Slater–Jastrow value and constituting the most precise QMC-based theoretical estimate for HEG reported here.

In finite periodic simulation cells using Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave functions (where the Jastrow and backflow factors each contain variational parameters), reoptimizing the Jastrow and backflow parameters for an excited-state configuration (an excitation produced by adding or removing an electron in a particular Bloch state) can lower the total DMC energy in that finite cell—for example, removing an electron from 𝗸=0 in a 26-electron paramagnetic 2D HEG at density parameter r_s=1 reduced the DMC total energy by 0.000854(4) hartree atomic units (a.u.)—but this finite-cell energy lowering is a manifestation of finite-size error and tends to reduce the computed excitation bandwidth and thus amplify finite-size bias in bandwidth-derived quantities such as the quasiparticle effective mass; therefore, to avoid introducing extra finite-size bias the Jastrow and backflow parameters are optimized in the ground state and then reused unchanged for excited-state energy evaluations.

Consider a two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG) in the thermodynamic limit defined by taking the particle number \\(N\\to\\infty\\) while holding the electron density fixed. Let the Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial many-electron wave function depend on finite-dimensional real parameter vectors \\(J\\) (the Jastrow-factor parameters controlling explicit two-body correlation terms, with polynomial and plane-wave expansions in interelectron distances cut off at the simulation-cell inscribed-circle radius) and \\(B\\) (the backflow-function parameters controlling polynomial backflow coordinate transformations). The proposition is: creating a single-particle excitation by adding or removing one electron at a definite wavevector \\(\\mathbf{k}\\) (changing the occupation by one at that \\(\\mathbf{k}\\) while preserving the thermodynamic-limit density) leaves the values of the optimal correlation-parameter vectors \\((J,B)\\) that minimize the ground-state energy per particle unchanged to leading order; equivalently, a single isolated quasiparticle excitation has a negligible effect on the optimal short- and medium-range two-body correlation functions and backflow transformations in the infinite 2D HEG.

Define backflow as the transformation of electronic coordinates $\mathbf{r}_{i}\mapsto\tilde{\mathbf{r}}_{i}=\mathbf{r}_{i}+\sum_{j\neq i}\eta(|\mathbf{r}_{i}-\mathbf{r}_{j}|)(\mathbf{r}_{i}-\mathbf{r}_{j})$ where $\eta(\cdot)$ is a short-range polynomial backflow function; the proposition is that including such backflow functions in Slater-Jastrow trial wave functions changes the fixed-node DMC total energy per electron by a substantial amount (improving the nodal surface) but alters the momentum-density distribution $\rho(\mathbf{k})$ only slightly (for example, by a small transfer of spectral weight above the Fermi wave vector) for paramagnetic 2D HEG densities in the range studied.

For the homogeneous electron gas at density parameter $r_{s}=3.99$, diffusion QMC calculations using Slater–Jastrow backflow (BF) trial wavefunctions provide an improved description of many-body correlations compared to plain Slater–Jastrow trial functions, and the reported BF QMC value $Z_{k_{F}}^{\mathrm{HEG}}=0.66(2)$ is taken as a reliable high-accuracy reference for the quasiparticle renormalization factor at this density within the quoted uncertainty.

Define a Slater–Jastrow–backflow (SJB) trial wave function for an N-electron two-dimensional uniform electron liquid (2D-UEL) with electron coordinates {r_i}_{i=1}^N as
      Ψ_SJB({r_i}) = e^{J({r_i})} · det[φ_t(X_j)],
      where the backflow-shifted coordinates are X_i = r_i + ξ_i({r_j}) with each backflow displacement ξ_i({r_j}) represented by a polynomial (or parametrized function) of interelectronic separations, and J is a Jastrow correlation factor. In fixed-node diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) the projected energy and derived excitation energies are constrained by the nodal surface of det[φ_t(X_j)]; including the backflow transformation therefore materially modifies the nodal surface relative to the bare Slater determinant (SJ) and can substantially reduce fixed-node variational bias in quasiparticle energies and their derivatives, producing noticeable changes in the extracted effective mass m* as observed in direct SJ versus SJB comparisons.
      P. López Ríos et al., Inhomogeneous backflow transformations in quantum Monte Carlo calculations, Phys. Rev. E 74, 066701 (2006).

Let a Slater-Jastrow-backflow (SJB) trial wave function be defined as $\Psi_{\mathrm{SJB}}(\mathbf{R}) = J(\mathbf{R})\,\det[\phi_{i}(\tilde{\mathbf{r}}_{j})]$ where $J(\mathbf{R})$ is a Jastrow correlation factor (a symmetric positive function built from polynomial and plane-wave electron-electron terms), $\tilde{\mathbf{r}}_{j}$ are quasiparticle coordinates obtained by backflow coordinate transforms that are polynomial functions of electron separations, and the fixed-node DMC energy is computed by constraining the nodal surface of the propagated wave function to equal that of $\Psi_{\mathrm{SJB}}$. The nodal surface produced by SJB wave functions is assumed to be sufficiently close to the exact fermionic ground-state nodal surface that the resulting fixed-node DMC energy per electron differs from the true ground-state energy by an amount smaller than or comparable to the quoted systematic and statistical uncertainties in high-quality SJB-DMC calculations for the 2D HEG.
      P. López Ríos et al., Phys. Rev. E 74, 066701 (2006); N. D. Drummond et al., Phys. Rev. B 70, 235119 (2004).
